knowledge.title: VRAM is the primary local AI constraint
knowledge.content: Local LLM inference and diffusion workloads keep model weights and intermediate data in GPU memory. 12GB suits smaller experiments, 16GB is a strong hobbyist target, and 24GB materially expands the models and batch sizes that fit without CPU offload.

knowledge.title: CUDA ecosystem preference
knowledge.content: Many common AI tools are optimized first for NVIDIA CUDA. AMD cards can offer strong raster value, but users who need broad framework compatibility should prioritize CUDA unless they have verified their exact stack.

knowledge.title: RTX 4090 local AI profile
knowledge.content: The RTX 4090 combines 24GB VRAM with CUDA support. It is a high-cost option best considered when local AI capacity matters more than power draw or compact size.

knowledge.title: RTX 5090 flagship profile
knowledge.content: The RTX 5090 combines 32GB GDDR7 with CUDA support and a 575W total graphics power rating. It requires an enthusiast budget, a compatible case, and at least a 1000W system power plan.

knowledge.title: RTX 4070 Ti Super balanced profile
knowledge.content: The RTX 4070 Ti Super provides 16GB VRAM and strong 1440p to 4K capability at lower power and cost than flagship GPUs. It is a balanced choice for mixed gaming, creative, and CUDA work.

knowledge.title: RTX 4070 Super gaming profile
knowledge.content: The RTX 4070 Super is efficient for high-quality 1440p gaming and supports CUDA, but its 12GB VRAM is less flexible for larger local AI workloads.

knowledge.title: RX 7900 XTX raster value
knowledge.content: The RX 7900 XTX offers 24GB VRAM and excellent high-resolution raster performance. It is compelling for 4K gaming when CUDA compatibility is not required.

knowledge.title: RX 7800 XT 1440p value
knowledge.content: The RX 7800 XT is a strong value target for 1440p gaming with 16GB VRAM. It favors raster performance per dollar over CUDA ecosystem support.

knowledge.title: Ryzen 7 9800X3D gaming focus
knowledge.content: The Ryzen 7 9800X3D is an enthusiast gaming CPU. Its value is strongest in high-refresh gaming systems where the GPU is already well funded.

knowledge.title: Ryzen 7 7800X3D gaming profile
knowledge.content: The Ryzen 7 7800X3D remains a focused gaming option with lower platform power than many high-core-count alternatives. It is less compelling for heavily threaded workstation tasks.

knowledge.title: Ryzen 9 7900X workstation profile
knowledge.content: Twelve cores and strong productivity performance make the Ryzen 9 7900X suitable for compilation, rendering, containers, and mixed workstation use. It requires stronger cooling than 65W parts.

knowledge.title: Ryzen 7 7700 balanced profile
knowledge.content: The Ryzen 7 7700 combines eight cores with a 65W rating. It is a quiet, efficient foundation for development and balanced builds with a useful AM5 upgrade path.

knowledge.title: Ryzen 5 7600 value profile
knowledge.content: The Ryzen 5 7600 preserves AM5 upgradeability while leaving more of a constrained gaming budget for the GPU.

knowledge.title: Core i7-14700K productivity profile
knowledge.content: The Core i7-14700K offers high multithreaded throughput for editing and compilation, but its peak power and cooling requirements are substantial.

knowledge.title: Memory capacity for development
knowledge.content: Docker, local databases, IDEs, browsers, and emulators compete for memory. 32GB is a practical baseline; 64GB provides meaningful headroom for heavier container and mobile build workflows.

knowledge.title: System RAM for local AI
knowledge.content: System RAM does not replace VRAM, but 64GB or more helps with model loading, CPU offload, data preparation, and running supporting services beside a local model.

knowledge.title: 64GB DDR5 workload fit
knowledge.content: A 64GB DDR5 kit is the default workstation capacity for local AI, development, and video editing when the budget allows.

knowledge.title: 32GB DDR5 value fit
knowledge.content: A 32GB DDR5 kit is sufficient for gaming and general productivity, and it protects budget for the GPU in performance-focused gaming builds.

knowledge.title: Power supply headroom rule
knowledge.content: A power supply should exceed estimated sustained system draw by at least 35 percent. Additional headroom can reduce fan noise and preserve room for future GPU upgrades.

knowledge.title: 850W Gold mainstream high-end fit
knowledge.content: An 850W Gold PSU is a common fit for high-end single-GPU systems when it satisfies the 1.35x power rule. It offers more upgrade margin than 750W without flagship PSU cost.

knowledge.title: 1000W Gold enthusiast fit
knowledge.content: A 1000W Gold PSU is appropriate for higher-draw GPUs and workstation CPUs where 850W would leave limited transient or upgrade headroom.

knowledge.title: Case clearance constraints
knowledge.content: GPU length, air-cooler height, motherboard form factor, and PSU form factor are hard physical constraints. These values must be checked deterministically before a case is accepted.

knowledge.title: Fractal North balanced airflow
knowledge.content: The Fractal North supports full-size ATX components with 355mm GPU clearance and 170mm air-cooler clearance. It balances airflow, acoustics, and upgrade room.

knowledge.title: NR200P small-form-factor constraints
knowledge.content: The NR200P is a Mini-ITX enclosure that requires an SFX power supply and limits tower cooler height. Compact builds require stricter part filtering and usually carry a price premium.

knowledge.title: Cooling capacity rule
knowledge.content: A cooler's thermal rating must meet or exceed the CPU power rating. Quiet builds benefit from additional capacity because the cooler can dissipate heat at lower fan speed.

knowledge.title: Peerless Assassin value cooling
knowledge.content: The Peerless Assassin 120 SE offers high air-cooling capacity at low cost. It is well suited to mainstream and many high-tier CPUs when case height allows it.

knowledge.title: 360mm AIO high-power cooling
knowledge.content: A 360mm AIO provides additional thermal capacity for high-power workstation CPUs, but costs more and requires a compatible radiator mount.

knowledge.title: 1080p gaming allocation
knowledge.content: At 1080p and high refresh rates, CPU frame delivery matters more than at higher resolutions. GPU budget remains important, but extreme GPU spending can produce diminishing returns.

knowledge.title: 1440p gaming allocation
knowledge.content: 1440p gaming usually benefits from a GPU-first budget with a balanced modern CPU. It is the broadest value target for high-refresh PC gaming.

knowledge.title: 4K gaming allocation
knowledge.content: At 4K the GPU is normally the primary performance constraint. Budget should favor the graphics card while retaining enough CPU, cooling, and PSU quality to avoid platform imbalance.

knowledge.title: NVMe capacity for workstation use
knowledge.content: Development and editing systems benefit from 2TB or more of fast NVMe storage for repositories, containers, caches, footage, and active projects.

knowledge.title: AM5 platform upgradeability
knowledge.content: AM5 boards provide a clearer CPU upgrade path than mature end-of-cycle platforms. The value is highest when the user explicitly plans a future CPU upgrade.

knowledge.title: B650 platform fit
knowledge.content: A well-equipped B650 motherboard is a sensible mainstream AM5 choice with DDR5 and multiple NVMe slots. Enthusiast CPUs may justify stronger power delivery or an X670E board.

knowledge.title: X670E workstation expansion
knowledge.content: X670E boards add expansion and connectivity for high-tier AM5 workstations. They should be selected for actual I/O or upgrade needs rather than prestige alone.

knowledge.title: Core i7-14700K high-refresh gaming profile
knowledge.content: The Core i7-14700K is an Intel LGA1700 option for high-refresh gaming and mixed productivity. Its high peak power requires substantial cooling, and the mature socket has less future CPU upgrade room than newer platforms.

knowledge.title: White Z790 platform pairing
knowledge.content: A Z790 DDR5 motherboard is the appropriate enthusiast chipset pairing for an unlocked Intel LGA1700 CPU. White or silver heatsink variants can satisfy a light-color build without changing socket or memory compatibility.

knowledge.title: Planning a white RGB build
knowledge.content: A coherent white build should prioritize visible components such as the case, graphics card, motherboard heatsinks, memory, and cooler. RGB is an appearance preference and must never override socket, clearance, cooling, or PSU safety rules.

knowledge.title: 4K 240Hz target trade-off
knowledge.content: A 4K 240Hz target is exceptionally demanding and depends heavily on the game, settings, and upscaling. The recommendation should maximize GPU headroom without claiming that every title will sustain 240 FPS.

knowledge.title: RTX 5080 4K and creator profile
knowledge.content: The GeForce RTX 5080 reference design combines 16GB GDDR7, CUDA support, a 360W total graphics power rating, and Blackwell media/AI features. It is a high-end 4K and creator option below the RTX 5090, but 16GB VRAM is a meaningful limit for larger local AI models.

knowledge.title: RTX 5070 Ti balanced high-end profile
knowledge.content: The GeForce RTX 5070 Ti provides 16GB GDDR7 and CUDA at a 300W reference power target. It is positioned for high-refresh 1440p, entry 4K, creator acceleration, and moderate local AI workloads without flagship cost.

knowledge.title: RTX 5060 Ti 16GB capacity-value trade-off
knowledge.content: The 16GB RTX 5060 Ti offers more model and texture capacity than 8GB cards while retaining CUDA support and a 180W reference power target. Its compute throughput is midrange, so capacity-sensitive hobby AI can fit even when raw generation or training speed is below higher-tier GPUs.

knowledge.title: Radeon RX 9070 XT RDNA 4 profile
knowledge.content: The Radeon RX 9070 XT has 16GB GDDR6, a 304W typical board power rating, and a 750W minimum PSU recommendation from AMD. It is a strong raster and FSR-oriented 4K gaming option, but users requiring CUDA-first software should still prefer NVIDIA.

knowledge.title: Intel Arc B580 value profile
knowledge.content: Intel Arc B580 Limited Edition provides 12GB VRAM, 190W total board power, AV1 media support, and a $249 recommended customer price. It is relevant to value 1080p/1440p builds, while application and game-specific driver behavior should be verified for specialized workloads.
knowledge.sourceTitle: Intel Arc B580 product specifications and ordering

knowledge.title: Ryzen 9 9950X3D2 developer workstation profile
knowledge.content: Ryzen 9 9950X3D2 combines 16 cores, 32 threads, 208MB total cache, a 200W TDP, and an $899 SEP. It targets high-budget development, compilation, simulation, creation, and gaming systems where both cache capacity and core throughput matter.

knowledge.title: Ryzen 9 9950X3D mixed gaming and creation profile
knowledge.content: Ryzen 9 9950X3D combines 16 Zen 5 cores with second-generation 3D V-Cache on AM5. It fits premium systems that need strong gaming latency and high multicore throughput, but it should not displace cheaper eight-core X3D options in gaming-only budgets.

knowledge.title: Core Ultra 7 265K LGA1851 profile
knowledge.content: Core Ultra 7 265K has 20 cores and 20 threads, up to 5.5GHz turbo, 125W base power, and 250W maximum turbo power on LGA1851. It is suited to Intel-preference mixed productivity builds but requires a Z890-class motherboard and strong cooling at unrestricted power.

knowledge.title: AM5 B850 and X870 platform choice
knowledge.content: B850 boards can provide PCIe 5.0 storage and mainstream connectivity, while X870 boards add mandatory USB4-class connectivity and often more high-speed I/O. Board selection should follow actual M.2, USB4, LAN, Wi-Fi, and expansion needs rather than chipset prestige.

knowledge.title: LGA1851 and Z890 compatibility boundary
knowledge.content: Intel Core Ultra 200S desktop processors use LGA1851 and are not compatible with LGA1700 Z790 or B760 motherboards. The motherboard filter must keep LGA1851 CPUs on compatible 800-series Intel boards such as Z890.
knowledge.sourceTitle: Intel Core Ultra desktop processor product brief

knowledge.title: PCIe 5.0 SSD performance and thermal trade-off
knowledge.content: High-end PCIe 5.0 SSDs such as Samsung 9100 Pro can exceed 14GB/s sequential reads, but cost and heat are materially higher than mature PCIe 4.0 drives. Gaming load times rarely justify allocating budget away from the GPU solely for peak sequential throughput; large creator and data workflows benefit more.

knowledge.title: ATX 3.1 and modern GPU power planning
knowledge.content: For high-draw current GPUs, an ATX 3.1 supply with a native 12V-2x6 cable simplifies cabling and is designed around modern transient requirements. Wattage still must satisfy deterministic sustained-load headroom; the connector standard does not make an undersized PSU acceptable.

knowledge.title: White dual-chamber showcase case planning
knowledge.content: A white dual-chamber case can provide panoramic presentation, cable separation, and extensive radiator support, but it is physically larger than a conventional mid-tower. Visible-component color matching should remain a preference layer after GPU length, cooler/radiator, motherboard, and PSU compatibility checks.
knowledge.sourceTitle: NZXT H9 Flow RGB official product specifications

knowledge.title: Small-form-factor cost and cooling trade-off
knowledge.content: Mini-ITX systems typically require a Mini-ITX motherboard and SFX power supply and may sharply limit air-cooler height and GPU dimensions. A compact request should trigger hard form-factor filtering and should expect higher component cost or reduced thermal headroom.
